author: Hippolytus of Rome
work: Against the Heresy of One Noetus
translation: S. D. F. Salmond (ANF vol. 5), public domain
note: Hippolytus writing AGAINST Noetus the modalist (c. AD 210): the Father, Son and Spirit are really distinct, yet there is one God — 'I shall not indeed speak of two Gods, but of one.' A century before Nicaea, John 1:1 is read as confessing the Word AS God. DELICACY: 'The Father decrees, the Word executes' and 'It is the Father who commands, and the Son who obeys' are pre-Nicene ECONOMY/taxis language — they order the Persons' roles in the one divine work, NOT their being; Hippolytus's whole argument is that the distinction of Persons does not divide the one God. Never quote either clause without its resolving companions, both of which are inside this excerpt: 'The economy of harmony is led back to one God; for God is One' and 'we cannot otherwise think of one God, but by believing in truth in Father and Son and Holy Spirit.' Likewise 'of two Persons however, and of a third economy' is pre-Nicene idiom predating settled Trinitarian vocabulary — read it against the chapter's own close, 'it is through this Trinity that the Father is glorified.' Verified verbatim against two independent PD hosts (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20); the John 1:1 inner quotation marks are restored as ANF prints them. SOURCING NOTE: cite the Wikisource/CCEL ANF page, NOT New Advent 0521 — New Advent applies a site-wide Hippolytus boilerplate crediting J. H. MacMahon, who translated only the *Refutation of All Heresies*; ANF vol. 5 heads this section 'Translated by the Rev. S. D. F. Salmond,' which is the correct credit used here. (ANF also italicises 'above all / through all / in all'; italics are not carried in this plain-text corpus.)
text: And the blessed John, in the testimony of his Gospel, gives us an account of this economy (disposition) and acknowledges this Word as God, when he says, "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God." If, then, the Word was with God, and was also God, what follows? Would one say that he speaks of two Gods? I shall not indeed speak of two Gods, but of one; of two Persons however, and of a third economy (disposition), viz., the grace of the Holy Ghost. For the Father indeed is One, but there are two Persons, because there is also the Son; and then there is the third, the Holy Spirit. The Father decrees, the Word executes, and the Son is manifested, through whom the Father is believed on. The economy of harmony is led back to one God; for God is One. It is the Father who commands, and the Son who obeys, and the Holy Spirit who gives understanding: the Father who is above all, and the Son who is through all, and the Holy Spirit who is in all. And we cannot otherwise think of one God, but by believing in truth in Father and Son and Holy Spirit.
